title: TEC Prediction with GNSS–Aditya-L1 & LSTM
desc: Time-series prediction of ionospheric TEC using GNSS and Aditya-L1 solar data.
content:
Overview

This project develops a data-driven framework to predict ionospheric Total Electron Content (TEC) by integrating GNSS observations with solar activity data from Aditya-L1. The work focuses on understanding the nonlinear coupling between solar dynamics and ionospheric behaviour using both statistical analysis and deep learning.

Problem

Ionospheric TEC variations significantly affect GNSS signal accuracy, causing delays and positioning errors in navigation systems. Traditional models lack the ability to capture short-term fluctuations driven by solar events such as flares and solar wind disturbances, making real-time prediction challenging.

Data & Preprocessing

  • GNSS data from AAI stations (Ahmedabad, Trivandrum, Shimla)
  • STEC computed from dual-frequency measurements and converted to VTEC using mapping functions
  • Elevation filtering (>30°) and median aggregation across satellites for noise reduction
  • Solar data from Aditya-L1:
    • SOLEXS: X-ray photon flux (2–22 keV)
    • ASPEX: Ion flux (TH1, TH2) and plasma parameters
  • Time synchronization across datasets with ±5s tolerance
  • Interpolation and cleaning for continuous time-series generation

Methodology

  • Correlation analysis using Spearman and Mutual Information
  • Feature engineering with temporal inputs
  • 24-timestep sequence modeling
  • LSTM (32 units) + Dropout + Dense layers

Results

  • Ahmedabad: RMSE ≈ 3.61 TECU, MAPE ≈ 7.25%
  • Shimla: RMSE ≈ 2.77 TECU, MAPE ≈ 6.68%
  • Trivandrum: RMSE ≈ 1.91 TECU, MAPE ≈ 3.69%
  • Robust under 10–50% noise
  • Cross-location generalization achieved (MAPE ≈ 15%)

Key Contributions

  • End-to-end TEC prediction pipeline
  • Fusion of GNSS and Aditya-L1 solar data
  • Deep learning-based ionospheric modeling
  • Robustness and generalization validation

Tech Stack

Python • TensorFlow/Keras • Pandas • NumPy • CDFlib • Astropy

title: Extended Kalman Filter for GPS Positioning
desc: State estimation and noise-aware GPS positioning using EKF.
content:
Overview

This project develops an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) framework for GPS-based positioning by modeling nonlinear satellite–receiver dynamics and measurement uncertainties. The work focuses on recursive state estimation under varying motion conditions, including stationary, low-dynamic, and high-dynamic scenarios.

Problem

GPS positioning relies on nonlinear pseudorange measurements affected by multiple sources of error such as atmospheric delay, clock bias, multipath effects, and measurement noise. Classical linear methods fail to accurately estimate receiver position and velocity in dynamic environments, requiring a robust nonlinear estimation approach.

Modeling

  • Nonlinear pseudorange equations based on satellite–receiver geometry
  • Linearization using Jacobian matrices
  • State vector including position, velocity, clock bias, and clock drift
  • Dynamic models for stationary, low-dynamic, and high-dynamic systems

Methodology

  • Initialization using least squares / Gauss–Newton method
  • State prediction using system dynamics (F matrix)
  • Measurement update using observation model (H matrix)
  • Covariance propagation and Kalman gain computation
  • Recursive prediction–update cycle

Noise & Uncertainty

  • Process noise modeling using covariance matrices
  • Measurement noise from atmospheric and receiver errors
  • Clock bias modeled as stochastic process
  • Allan variance for realistic clock behavior

Results

  • Accurate position and velocity estimation
  • Stable convergence under noisy conditions
  • Robust performance across dynamic scenarios
  • Effective handling of nonlinear GPS measurements

Tech Stack

Python • Kalman Filtering • State Estimation • Linear Algebra • Numerical Methods

title: Lunar Mission Design & Space Systems Simulation
desc: Mission planning, orbital mechanics, propulsion analysis, and lunar payload design using numerical simulations.
content:
Overview

This project was carried out during the Virtual Training and Internship Program (VTIP) by STAR Labs, focused on Space Technology and Aeronautical Rocketry. The work involved studying rocket propulsion, orbital mechanics, spacecraft dynamics, and mission planning through numerical simulations and engineering-based problem solving. The internship concluded with a complete lunar mission architecture involving Earth launch, parking orbit insertion, Trans-Lunar Injection (TLI), lunar landing simulations, and payload design concepts for future lunar exploration.

Rocket Physics & Dynamics

  • Newtonian mechanics and conservation of momentum in rocket propulsion
  • Aerodynamic drag modeling and atmospheric dynamics
  • Rocket structure, staging, and propulsion systems
  • Specific impulse, exhaust velocity, and rocket equation derivations
  • Orbital velocity and maneuver calculations

Numerical Simulations

  • Launch vehicle ascent simulations using numerical integration
  • Altitude, velocity, acceleration, drag, and fuel consumption analysis
  • Two-stage rocket modeling under varying atmospheric conditions
  • Earth parking orbit insertion and trajectory calculations
  • Simulation of orbital raising maneuvers and Trans-Lunar Injection

Lunar Mission Architecture

  • Launch from Earth into 300 km parking orbit
  • Orbit-raising maneuvers from low Earth orbit
  • Transfer trajectory from Earth to Moon
  • Lunar parking orbit insertion
  • Powered descent and lunar landing trajectory simulation

Payload & Research Concepts

  • Gene Bank concept for cryogenic biological preservation inside permanently shadowed lunar craters
  • 4-D Bioprinting system for future space medicine and autonomous biological fabrication
  • Bone Density Experiment studying effects of lunar microgravity and radiation on biological tissues
  • Payload architecture and scientific justification for lunar deployment

Tools & Technical Exposure

  • GMAT simulations for orbital mechanics and mission trajectory analysis
  • Numerical integration methods for spacecraft motion
  • Engineering calculations for propulsion and maneuver planning
  • Mission systems thinking and design methodology
  • Team-based aerospace systems presentation and technical reporting

Results

  • Successful simulation of multi-stage launch and lunar transfer mission
  • Modeling of lunar descent dynamics and powered landing trajectory
  • Development of conceptual lunar payload systems for long-term exploration
  • Comprehensive understanding of propulsion systems, orbital mechanics, and mission planning workflows

Tech Stack

Python • GMAT • Numerical Integration • Orbital Mechanics • Rocket Propulsion • Space Systems Engineering

title: Wavefunction Tunnelling & Trapping in a Finite Potential Well
desc: Numerical simulation of quantum tunnelling, interference, and bound-state trapping using the Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation.
content:
Overview

This project investigates the dynamics of Gaussian wave packets interacting with a one-dimensional finite potential well through numerical solutions of the Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE). Unlike the idealized infinite well, the finite well allows both bound and scattering states, enabling direct observation of quantum tunnelling, transmission, reflection, interference, and partial trapping of wavefunctions. The simulation models counter-propagating Gaussian packets interacting with the well and analyzes the resulting quantized energy distributions.

Quantum System

  • One-dimensional finite square potential well
  • Bound and scattering state behavior
  • Gaussian wave packet propagation
  • Reflection, transmission, and interference dynamics
  • Projection onto quantized bound eigenstates

Mathematical Framework

  • Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)
  • Finite-difference Hamiltonian construction
  • Crank–Nicolson numerical integration scheme
  • Eigenstate decomposition and occupation probability analysis
  • Expectation value calculations for trapped states

Methodology

  • Construction of finite square well potential
  • Generation of Gaussian wave packets from opposite directions
  • Numerical time evolution using Crank–Nicolson matrices
  • Tracking of probability density evolution over time
  • Projection of trapped wavefunction components onto discrete eigenstates

Simulation Results

  • Observation of quantum tunnelling through finite barriers
  • Partial reflection and transmission of wave packets
  • Strong standing-wave interference patterns inside the well
  • Residual trapped probability density after interaction
  • Emergence of quantized energy levels and bound-state occupations

Physical Interpretation

The simulations demonstrate how finite quantum systems naturally support both propagating and localized states. Counter-propagating wave packets produce constructive and destructive interference inside the well, while a fraction of the wavefunction remains confined within discrete energy levels. The project provides computational visualization of tunnelling, superposition, energy quantization, and wavefunction localization in finite quantum potentials.

Applications

  • Quantum Computing and Qubit Systems
  • Quantum Dots and Nanostructures
  • Tunnelling Devices and Josephson Junctions
  • Semiconductor Quantum Wells
  • Nuclear and Particle Physics

Tech Stack

Python • NumPy • Matplotlib • Computational Physics • Quantum Mechanics • Numerical Methods

title: Study of the Doppler Effect
desc: Experimental verification of Doppler frequency shifts using a rotating acoustic source and real-time frequency analysis.
content:
Overview

This project experimentally investigates the Doppler Effect using a rotating sound source and stationary receiver setup. By generating controlled relative motion between the source and observer, the experiment demonstrates measurable shifts in observed frequency as predicted by wave mechanics. The work combines theoretical analysis, rotational dynamics, experimental acoustics, and real-time frequency measurements to validate Doppler frequency transformations.

Experimental Setup

  • Rotating speaker mounted on a motor-driven disc
  • Stationary receiver using smartphone-based frequency analysis
  • Controlled rotational motion to simulate moving sound source
  • Measurement of frequency shifts during approaching and receding motion
  • Real-time acoustic spectrum analysis

Theoretical Framework

  • Doppler frequency transformation for moving sources
  • Wavefront compression and rarefaction analysis
  • Relative motion between source and observer
  • Linear velocity derivation from rotational motion
  • Comparison of theoretical and experimental frequency shifts

Methodology

  • Conversion of motor RPM into linear source velocity
  • Emission of fixed frequencies through rotating speaker
  • Recording frequency shifts for approaching and receding motion
  • Experimental measurements for 500 Hz, 800 Hz, and 1200 Hz signals
  • Error analysis against ideal Doppler predictions

Results & Analysis

  • Successful observation of Doppler frequency increase during source approach
  • Observed reduction in frequency when source moved away
  • Experimental results closely matched theoretical predictions
  • Average experimental error below 1%
  • Verification of acoustic Doppler transformations under rotational motion

Physical Interpretation

The experiment demonstrates how relative motion alters perceived wave frequency through compression and expansion of wavefronts. Rotational motion of the source produced measurable Doppler shifts that quantitatively matched theoretical expectations, validating the classical Doppler model for acoustic waves. The project also highlights the broader significance of Doppler physics in astronomy, radar systems, and wave-based sensing technologies.

Applications

  • Astronomical Redshift & Blueshift Measurements
  • Radar and Velocity Detection Systems
  • Medical Doppler Ultrasound Imaging
  • Wave Propagation and Acoustic Engineering
  • Signal Processing and Motion Sensing

Tech Stack

Experimental Physics • Acoustics • Rotational Dynamics • Signal Analysis • Wave Mechanics • Data Interpretation

title: Mechanical Equivalent of Heat using Thermocouple & Arduino
desc: Automated calorimetry experiment integrating thermocouples, operational amplifiers, and Arduino-based temperature acquisition.
content:
Overview

This project modernizes the classical Joule’s Mechanical Equivalent of Heat experiment by integrating embedded electronics, thermocouple sensing, operational amplification, and Arduino-based data acquisition. The system continuously measures temperature rise in a calorimeter during electrical heating and computes the conversion between electrical energy and thermal energy using automated real-time sensing and radiation-corrected analysis.

Experimental System

  • Calorimeter with resistance heating coil
  • K-type thermocouple for temperature sensing
  • Inverting operational amplifier for signal amplification
  • Arduino Uno for real-time data acquisition
  • Continuous temperature logging and processing

Physical Principles

  • Electrical-to-thermal energy conversion
  • Joule heating and calorimetry
  • Seebeck effect in thermocouples
  • Operational amplifier signal amplification
  • Radiation correction using cooling curves

Methodology

  • Electrical heating of a water calorimeter using controlled current
  • Measurement of thermocouple voltage generated from temperature rise
  • Amplification of millivolt-level thermocouple signals using op-amp circuitry
  • Analog-to-digital conversion using Arduino Uno ADC
  • Continuous logging of temperature data at 1-second intervals
  • Application of radiation correction through cooling slope analysis

Data Analysis

  • Generation of temperature vs time heating and cooling curves
  • Voltage-to-temperature conversion using Seebeck relations
  • Radiation correction using cooling-rate interpolation
  • Calculation of electrical energy supplied and heat absorbed
  • Experimental determination of Joule’s constant (J)

Results

  • Successful automated temperature acquisition using Arduino and thermocouple integration
  • Continuous real-time monitoring of calorimeter heating dynamics
  • Experimental value of Joule’s constant measured as 3.79 J/cal
  • Percentage error of approximately 9.46% compared to accepted value
  • Improved precision through automated data logging and radiation correction

Physical Interpretation

The experiment demonstrates the equivalence between electrical energy and thermal energy through modernized calorimetric instrumentation. Integration of thermocouples, analog amplification, and microcontroller-based acquisition significantly improved measurement continuity and reduced human error compared to traditional manual calorimetry methods. The project also highlights the practical application of embedded systems in experimental physics and thermal measurement technologies.

Applications

  • Industrial Calorimetry Systems
  • Embedded Temperature Monitoring
  • Thermal Instrumentation and Automation
  • Energy Conversion Studies
  • Material Thermal Property Analysis

Tech Stack

Arduino Uno • Operational Amplifiers • Thermocouples • Embedded Systems • Calorimetry • Experimental Physics

title: Resonance in a Series LCR Circuit
desc: Experimental and theoretical analysis of resonance, impedance, and current response in a series LCR circuit.
content:
Overview

This project investigates the resonance behavior of a series LCR circuit under alternating current (AC) excitation. By analyzing the interaction between inductive reactance, capacitive reactance, and resistance, the experiment demonstrates how resonance leads to minimum impedance and maximum current flow. The work combines theoretical modeling, frequency-domain analysis, electrical measurements, and graphical interpretation of resonance characteristics.

Electrical System

  • Series-connected inductor, capacitor, and resistor
  • AC signal generator as frequency-variable input source
  • Digital multimeter-based voltage measurements
  • Frequency sweep across low to high frequency ranges
  • Observation of resonance and impedance variation

Theoretical Framework

  • Inductive reactance and capacitive reactance analysis
  • Frequency-dependent impedance calculations
  • Current response using Ohm’s law in AC circuits
  • Resonance condition where XL = XC
  • Resonant frequency derivation using LCR parameters

Methodology

  • Construction of a series LCR circuit using discrete components
  • Calculation of theoretical resonance frequency
  • Frequency sweep using function generator input
  • Measurement of voltage across the resistor at varying frequencies
  • Calculation of current values and resonance curves from experimental data

Experimental Analysis

  • Generation of frequency vs voltage response curves
  • Plotting of current-frequency resonance characteristics
  • Identification of resonance peak near calculated resonant frequency
  • Log-scale graphical analysis of circuit response
  • Comparison between theoretical and measured resonance behavior

Results

  • Successful observation of resonance in a series LCR circuit
  • Maximum current flow observed near 2 kHz resonant frequency
  • Minimum impedance achieved at resonance condition
  • Experimental resonance closely matched theoretical predictions
  • Clear resonance peak demonstrated through graphical analysis

Physical Interpretation

The experiment demonstrates how inductive and capacitive reactances dynamically balance in AC circuits. At resonance, energy oscillates efficiently between the electric field of the capacitor and the magnetic field of the inductor, minimizing overall impedance and maximizing current flow. The project highlights the foundational role of resonance phenomena in communication systems, filters, oscillators, and frequency-selective electronics.

Applications

  • Radio Frequency Tuning Circuits
  • Electronic Filters and Signal Processing
  • Oscillators and Communication Systems
  • Power Electronics and Frequency Control
  • AC Circuit Design and Analysis

Tech Stack

AC Circuit Analysis • Resonance Physics • Signal Generators • Experimental Electronics • Data Visualization • Electrical Measurements
